narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and middle-class family background, your law school application stood out in some ways, but the admissions model focused on key predictors of bar exam success. The model estimates a 79.1% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—schools use this to prioritize students likely to pass and build strong reputations. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, meaning your grades placed you in the bottom 10% of your peers that year, far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack average. This low performance carried the most weight because it signals potential challenges in handling rigorous legal coursework leading up to the bar. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile also at 1, again putting you in the lowest tier compared to most students' average around the 5th or 6th decile, reinforcing concerns about consistent academic strength from the start. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA actually nudged the prediction slightly toward passing success, as it's close to the dataset average of 3.21 and shows you can perform well in college-level work. Other elements like your low LSAT score of 27—well below the usual 37—and your plan to attend full-time, which is common for 93% of students, played supporting roles but weren't the top drivers. Compared to typical applicants who pass the bar, your early and later high school deciles are unusually low, which overshadowed strengths like your GPA. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income home—many in similar situations succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, seeking tutoring or academic coaching to strengthen your decile rankings in future terms, and exploring part-time options or gap-year prep programs that have helped others in your position. Reapplying after addressing these could shift the model's view in your favor. You're capable of turning this around with focused effort.
